target.title: refactor: internalize OpenClaw agent runtime
target.body:
## Summary

This PR removes the old Pi-shaped agent/runtime architecture from OpenClaw and re-integrates that functionality as OpenClaw-owned core, plugin, and SDK surfaces.

The main result is that OpenClaw no longer treats Pi as an embedded dependency or organizing concept. Agent execution, model/provider handling, sessions, compaction, transcript storage, provider startup, plugin runtime loading, and docs/tests now use OpenClaw names, OpenClaw ownership boundaries, and OpenClaw package/runtime contracts.

At a high level this PR:

- Extracts an OpenClaw-owned `@openclaw/agent-core` package from the previously embedded agent runtime code.
- Renames Pi-era source modules, tests, config surfaces, docs, QA scenarios, and type shims into OpenClaw agent/runtime concepts.
- Removes legacy provider/model compatibility behavior that was only there because Pi used to be a dependency boundary.
- Moves provider-owned behavior and dependencies into the owning provider/plugin packages instead of keeping them in root/core.
- Consolidates duplicated session, transcript, compaction, ACPX, Google stream, JSON parser, and test helper logic.
- Keeps compatibility only where it is a real plugin/public boundary, and marks those compatibility paths as deprecated instead of letting them look like internal architecture.
- Updates license/notice text for the incorporated Pi-derived code now that the code is internalized.
- Updates documentation, QA scenarios, test shards, dependency ownership metadata, and package/build wiring to match the new architecture.

## Why

The previous structure still had Pi concepts in places where OpenClaw should own the abstraction directly:

- package names and type declarations still referred to Pi agent/core/coding-agent concepts
- model/provider code still had fallback and alias behavior inherited from the dependency relationship
- sessions and transcript writing had parallel stacks instead of one canonical OpenClaw path
- provider runtime and plugin runtime code carried compatibility branches for old Pi/plugin layouts
- root dependencies included provider-specific packages that should belong to provider plugins
- docs and QA scenarios taught users and maintainers a Pi-era architecture that no longer matched the product

That made the refactor look like a dependency shuffle instead of a real integration. This PR makes the structure look like Pi had never existed as a separate internal dependency: OpenClaw owns the runtime core, providers own provider behavior, plugins cross into core through the SDK/manifest contracts, and compatibility is explicit/deprecated at the public boundary only.

## Architectural Changes

### OpenClaw Agent Core

Adds a new workspace package at `packages/agent-core`.

This package now owns the reusable agent runtime primitives that were previously internalized from the Pi dependency shape:

- agent loop orchestration
- harness execution types
- message conversion helpers
- prompt template helpers
- compaction primitives
- branch summarization utilities
- session interfaces and storage abstractions
- JSONL and memory-backed session repositories
- shell output/truncation helpers
- runtime dependency injection points
- validation helpers
- Node runtime helpers

The package is exported as `@openclaw/agent-core` and wired into root package/build config, workspace config, TypeScript config, and package manager metadata.

Core OpenClaw code now imports the shared runtime contracts from this package instead of reaching through Pi-named local modules or dependency shims.

### Agent Runtime Naming And Ownership

Renames Pi-era agent files and tests under `src/agents` into OpenClaw-owned names.

Examples of the broad rename/integration:

- `pi-auth-*` became `agent-auth-*`
- `pi-bundle-*` became `agent-bundle-*`
- `pi-hooks` became `agent-hooks`
- `pi-settings` became `agent-settings`
- `pi-tools*` became `agent-tools*`
- `pi-project-settings*` became `agent-project-settings*`
- `pi-model-discovery*` became `agent-model-discovery*`
- `pi-agent-core.d.ts` became `agent-core.d.ts`

The goal is not just cosmetic. The renamed modules now sit behind OpenClaw runtime ownership:

- agent runtime ids are OpenClaw runtime ids
- agent settings are OpenClaw agent settings
- bundled MCP materialization is OpenClaw agent bundle materialization
- tool definitions and tool policies are OpenClaw agent tool contracts
- compaction hooks are OpenClaw agent hooks

### Runtime And Plugin Boundary Cleanup

The PR tightens the boundary between core and plugins.

Core remains plugin-agnostic and no longer carries provider/plugin behavior simply because the old dependency needed it. Provider-specific defaults, catalogs, auth choices, discovery, and stream behavior move toward provider-owned packages or focused SDK seams.

Important changes include:

- provider runtime selection no longer routes through legacy Pi runtime names
- old provider alias normalization paths are removed or moved to explicit command/provider-facing compatibility where needed
- provider discovery fallbacks that existed for the old dependency graph are removed
- provider auth bridge behavior that referenced Pi concepts is removed
- provider runtime model types are made more explicit
- runtime plugin loading now uses OpenClaw plugin/runtime contracts instead of Pi package graph assumptions
- startup model ownership moves to manifests/provider catalogs instead of a static root model catalog
- bundled plugin metadata, gateway startup plugin ids, and runtime plugin tests are updated accordingly

### Provider And Model Refactor

This PR removes the old static model-catalog shape from core and replaces it with OpenClaw/provider-owned registry behavior.

Key points:

- static generated model catalogs are removed from runtime ownership paths
- model registration and lookup go through OpenClaw LLM registry code
- provider model ownership is resolved through provider/plugin metadata rather than hard-coded Pi-era catalog assumptions
- GitHub Copilot policy models are sourced from the provider path rather than duplicated core state
- model selection tests are updated for OpenClaw provider ids and current provider ownership
- provider alias handling is narrowed to command/user-facing interpretation where still needed
- legacy provider compatibility paths are removed from hot runtime code

The new LLM area under `src/llm` owns core model/provider contracts and built-in provider implementations:

- API registries
- model registries
- image model registries
- model utilities
- provider stream wrappers
- provider OAuth helpers
- provider diagnostics/event streams
- provider JSON parsing/sanitization utilities
- built-in provider registration

### Provider Dependencies Move To Owning Extensions

Provider-only dependencies are moved out of root/core and into the owning provider/plugin packages.

The most visible case is AWS/Bedrock:

- AWS SDK dependencies move to the Amazon Bedrock extension packages.
- Bedrock provider stream/runtime behavior is owned by the Bedrock extension.
- Root package metadata and lock/shrinkwrap files are updated so root no longer pulls provider-only AWS dependencies.
- Dependency ownership metadata is updated to describe the new package boundary.

Other provider packages and shrinkwraps are updated similarly where dependencies were previously inherited from the Pi/root dependency shape.

### Session And Transcript Unification

This PR consolidates session storage and transcript writing around OpenClaw-owned config/session modules and the new agent-core session abstractions.

Changes include:

- session version ownership moves into config session code
- transcript append helpers are shared instead of duplicated
- transcript headers and JSONL writing are centralized
- session load/store code is updated to use OpenClaw session contracts
- agent session SDK surfaces are updated to point at OpenClaw-owned session types
- gateway/session and command/session tests are updated for the new session model

This is intended to remove the old split between a Pi session stack and an OpenClaw session stack.

### Compaction And Context Runtime Cleanup

Compaction and context-engine code is updated to use the OpenClaw agent-core/runtime shape.

The PR:

- moves shared compaction primitives into `packages/agent-core`
- removes Pi-specific compaction constants/names
- updates compaction docs and QA scenarios to OpenClaw terminology
- updates context-engine host compatibility references
- keeps compaction/session behavior wired through OpenClaw runtime contracts
- adjusts tests to validate the renamed runtime paths

### ACPX Runtime Turn Deduplication

The ACPX plugin had duplicated lazy turn/run-turn adapter logic in both registration and service paths.

This PR extracts that into `extensions/acpx/src/runtime-turn.ts` and updates both callers to use the shared helper.

That shared helper now owns:

- lazy runtime resolution for `startTurn`
- compatibility adaptation from legacy `runTurn` runtimes to `startTurn`
- queued event delivery for legacy run-turn streams
- terminal result conversion from ACP done/error events
- cancellation and stream-close behavior

The behavior stays equivalent, but the implementation is now one path.

### Google And Vertex Stream Projection Deduplication

`src/llm/providers/google.ts` and `src/llm/providers/google-vertex.ts` had near-identical streaming loops.

This PR moves the shared projection into `consumeGoogleGenerateContentStream(...)` in `src/llm/providers/google-shared.ts`.

The shared path handles:

- stream start/done events
- first response id retention
- text block start/delta/end events
- thinking block start/delta/end events
- thought signature retention
- function call/tool call projection
- duplicate or missing tool call id handling
- stop reason mapping
- forcing `toolUse` when tool calls are present
- usage metadata conversion
- cost calculation
- abort/error handling

A new focused test, `src/llm/providers/google-shared.test.ts`, locks the shared projection behavior across text, thinking, tool calls, response ids, stop reasons, and usage/cost accounting.

### Unsafe Integer JSON Parser Deduplication

Ollama had a local copy of the unsafe-integer-preserving JSON parser that already existed in core agent transport code.

This PR promotes that parser to a narrow generic SDK subpath:

- `src/plugin-sdk/json-unsafe-integers.ts`
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/json-unsafe-integers`

The Ollama extension now imports the shared parser through the SDK boundary instead of duplicating the implementation or reaching into core internals.

Package exports, SDK entrypoint metadata, SDK docs, and plugin SDK export checks are updated for the new subpath.

### Test Fixture Deduplication

`src/agents/sandbox/workspace-skills-bridge-readonly.test.ts` repeated the same remote shell runner fixture in multiple cases.

This PR extracts that fixture into one typed local helper inside the test file. The tests keep the same assertions, but the duplicate shell runner setup is gone.

### Duplicate Scan Cleanup

After the final cleanup pass, duplicate scanning reports zero clones across the configured duplicate scan targets:

- production: 0 clones
- tests: 0 clones
- src-mixed: 0 clones
- extensions-mixed: 0 clones
- test-mixed: 0 clones

This specifically removed the remaining ACPX, Google/Vertex, Ollama JSON parser, and sandbox test fixture duplicate reports.

## Plugin SDK And Compatibility

The plugin SDK is updated to expose only intentional OpenClaw seams.

Changes include:

- new `agent-core` and agent-session SDK forwarding surfaces
- provider/LLM SDK entrypoints for shared provider runtime contracts
- narrow provider stream/model helper updates
- `json-unsafe-integers` as a generic parser utility for plugin-owned runtime code
- SDK baseline/export metadata updates
- documentation updates for SDK subpaths and migration guidance

Compatibility is kept where it is a real public/plugin boundary, not where it was merely internal implementation convenience.

Old public compatibility paths are represented through explicit compatibility/deprecation metadata and tests instead of being treated as current internal architecture.

## Legacy Pi Cleanup

This PR removes Pi as an internal runtime/package concept.

Removed or renamed areas include:

- Pi runtime internals
- Pi package graph tests
- Pi model fixture naming
- Pi agent/core type names
- Pi auth/model discovery names
- Pi docs pages and scenario names
- Pi-specific config/test shard names
- Pi wording in user-facing docs where the subject is now OpenClaw agent runtime

Remaining compatibility is intentionally narrow and boundary-owned:

- public SDK compatibility/deprecation metadata remains where old external plugin imports may still exist
- doctor/migration code retains explicit deprecation handling for real shipped config states
- docs/license notices explain incorporated code provenance

## Documentation Changes

The documentation is updated to describe the new OpenClaw-owned architecture.

Major doc changes include:

- adds `docs/agent-runtime-architecture.md`
- renames `docs/pi-dev.md` to `docs/openclaw-agent-runtime.md`
- removes the old `docs/pi.md`
- updates agent runtime, agent loop, queue steering, model/provider, context engine, compaction, status, migration, and plugin architecture pages
- updates plugin bundle and SDK docs to reflect runtime ownership and plugin boundaries
- updates provider docs where provider behavior moved to provider/plugin ownership
- updates QA scenario names and docs away from Pi/Codex-specific legacy wording where OpenClaw runtime wording is now correct

## Licensing And Notices

Because this PR internalizes code that originated in the Pi dependency shape, it updates repository notices:

- `LICENSE` gets the relevant additional copyright/provenance text.
- `THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.md` records incorporated Pi-derived code.
- The changelog notes the agent runtime internalization/refactor.

## macOS App Cleanup

The macOS app no longer carries its own static model catalog loader path from the old model-catalog architecture.

Removed:

- `apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/ModelCatalogLoader.swift`
- `apps/macos/Tests/OpenClawIPCTests/ModelCatalogLoaderTests.swift`
- associated debug/constants wiring for that removed catalog path

The app should consume the current OpenClaw model/provider surfaces instead of maintaining a parallel static catalog loader.

## Build, Tooling, And CI Metadata

The PR updates build/test/tooling metadata to match the new package/runtime layout:

- workspace package metadata for `packages/agent-core`
- root package exports/dependencies
- package manager lock/shrinkwrap data
- `tsconfig` and `tsdown` package/build wiring
- plugin SDK entrypoint metadata and generated baseline hashes
- dependency ownership metadata
- test project/shard names
- CodeQL workflow/query references for renamed runtime boundaries
- docs/i18n and QA scenario references
- duplicate scan coverage remains valid

## Review Shape

The branch is organized as a stack of focused commits rather than one giant mechanical blob.

Commit themes:

- extract the OpenClaw agent core package
- remove Pi runtime internals
- document the OpenClaw agent runtime cleanup
- decouple and make agent core runtime explicit
- consolidate compaction/runtime/session handling
- move model/provider ownership out of static Pi-era paths
- remove provider Pi auth and legacy runtime selection paths
- trim provider legacy paths and discovery fallbacks
- move CLI runtime ownership to plugins
- drop core provider alias normalization
- dedupe remaining helper implementations

## Behavior Notes

Expected behavior should stay the same for normal OpenClaw users, except that internal ownership, docs, diagnostics, and developer-facing names now reflect OpenClaw instead of Pi.

Intentional behavior/architecture changes:

- provider-only dependencies are no longer root/core dependencies when a provider plugin owns them
- provider/model fallback paths that existed only for the old Pi dependency boundary are removed
- static root model catalog behavior is replaced by provider/manifest/runtime ownership
- internal callers are migrated to current OpenClaw SDK/runtime surfaces
- compatibility is treated as deprecated public boundary support, not internal architecture

## Verification

Behavior addressed: fully internalize the old Pi-shaped agent runtime into OpenClaw-owned core/plugin/SDK surfaces; remove Pi-era runtime/provider/session duplication; keep only explicit deprecated compatibility at public/plugin boundaries; confirm no duplicate scan findings remain.

Real environment tested: local macOS checkout on branch `refactor/openclaw-agent-runtime-cleanup` at pushed head `f650426d547d8d2b15d2cef3ca228dddd51dbdc3`; AWS Crabbox Linux runner for broad changed gate before the final rebase, provider `aws`, lease `cbx_9b165c987860`, run `run_958f0472af21`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/workspace-skills-bridge-readonly.test.ts src/llm/providers/google-shared.test.ts extensions/acpx/register.runtime.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/service.test.ts extensions/ollama/src/stream.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/api-baseline.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-message.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- `node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs`
- `node scripts/sync-plugin-sdk-exports.mjs --check`
- `node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm dup:check:json`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --no-web-search`
- Crabbox/AWS broad gate: `pnpm crabbox:run -- --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json --shell -- "env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=6 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 pnpm check:changed"`

Evidence after fix:

- focused Vitest: 8 files passed, 45 tests passed
- build: passed, including plugin SDK export verification
- plugin SDK subpath export check: passed
- plugin SDK package export sync check: passed
- extension/plugin SDK boundary check: passed
- whitespace diff check: passed
- duplicate scan: 0 clones in production, tests, src-mixed, extensions-mixed, and test-mixed
- autoreview: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`
- Crabbox/AWS `pnpm check:changed`: exit 0 on run `run_958f0472af21`, lease `cbx_9b165c987860`

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw builds with the new `@openclaw/agent-core` package and renamed runtime surfaces; focused ACPX/Ollama/Google/plugin-SDK/sandbox tests pass; SDK exports are consistent; plugin boundary guards pass; duplicate scanner reports zero clones across all configured targets; broad changed gate passed on AWS Crabbox before the final rebase; the branch is rebased onto current `origin/main` and pushed at `f650426d547d8d2b15d2cef3ca228dddd51dbdc3`.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 4 constrained provider capabilities
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR keeps WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles honest by making hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed constraints visible instead of pretending they are equivalent official bot APIs. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 4 - Constrained/device-bound provider capabilities - Role: Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. - Head: `0295ff8709` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `0295ff8709` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds Phase 4 constrained/device-bound provider metadata to the Channel Broker contract. - Covers WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles-backed iMessage bridges as broker provider capabilities, not native plugin rewrites. - Stacks on Phase 3 official/app capability matrix (#86164), Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86114 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86148, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds `constraints` and `badges` to broker platform/provider capabilities. - Models constrained provider facts such as: - `businessApi`, `cloudApi`, `providerHosted` - `deviceBound`, `linkedDevice`, `qrPairing`, `sessionFragile` - `selfHosted`, `phoneNumberRequired`, `signalCli` - `macHostRequired`, `messagesSignedIn`, `privateApiOptional`, `privateApiRequired` - `externalBridge` - Exposes resolved provider capabilities in channel-broker status snapshots so UI/status can show constrained-provider badges. - Adds WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage target proof through `channel-broker`. - Documents constrained provider expectations in Channel Broker, SDK, WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles migration docs. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Constrained providers expose hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed limits through broker `constraints`/`badges`, and WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage still route through provider-owned broker requests. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that evaluated constrained-provider capability support and called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` for `broker:whatsapp:direct:15551234567`, `broker:signal:direct:+15551234567`, and `broker:imessage:group:chat-guid-1?threadId=imessage-thread-1`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "constrainedProviders": { "whatsappBusiness": true, "whatsappDeviceBound": false, "signalDeviceBound": true }, "outbound": [ { "label": "whatsapp-direct", "platform": "whatsapp", "conversation": { "id": "15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "signal-direct", "platform": "signal", "conversation": { "id": "+15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "imessage-group", "platform": "imessage", "conversation": { "id": "chat-guid-1", "type": "group", "threadId": "imessage-thread-1" } } ] } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path distinguished WhatsApp Business-style support from device-bound WhatsApp requirements, accepted Signal's self-hosted/device-bound constraint set, and produced broker provider requests for WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage. - **What was not tested:** Live WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, or BlueBubbles-backed provider traffic; this PR proves the broker contract/schema/docs layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/status.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - This PR does not implement Phase 5 legacy-plugin freeze. - Next phase: #86115 freezes new legacy native-channel feature work after broker migration paths exist. This PR supplies the constrained-provider metadata needed before that freeze can be credible.

target.title: feat(browser): expand Chrome MCP web capabilities
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Browser plugin's Chrome MCP existing-session transport only exposed a small subset of the browser automation and debug surface that was available in the local testbed, so real browser workflows still fell back to partial behavior.
- Solution: route the Chrome MCP transport through the richer Browser tool surface, including console/network diagnostics, text and coordinate interactions, dialogs, emulation, tracing, heap snapshots, Lighthouse, screencasts, extension inventory, and safer existing-session cleanup.
- What changed: updates are scoped to Browser plugin runtime/tests under `extensions/browser/...`; focused coverage was added for Chrome MCP routing, existing-session behavior, wrapper forwarding, live-surface behavior, CDP helpers, and profile cleanup ownership.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not modify live OpenClaw config, Gateway service config, credentials, Microsoft Teams/M365 tenant configuration, personal Chrome profiles, or `CHANGELOG.md`.

AI-assisted: yes. I understand the Browser plugin changes in this PR and ran the real behavior proof and local validation described below.

## Motivation

- This makes the Browser plugin's Chrome MCP existing-session mode useful for full web workflows instead of only basic navigation/click/readback.
- It also tightens cleanup behavior so dedicated OpenClaw-managed profiles can be stopped safely without targeting arbitrary personal or custom Chrome profiles.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix

commands.author: silvesterxm

target.title: fix(agents): recover DeepSeek DSML tool markup into synthetic tool calls
target.body:
## Summary
`microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro` can emit DSML tool markup as plain assistant text on the `openai-completions` route instead of native `tool_calls`. This PR adds a conservative recovery path that turns complete, valid DSML `invoke` blocks into synthetic `toolCall` blocks while preserving surrounding visible text and emitting every valid invoke in the response.

This branch now also includes the missing Foundry fallback fix that stops the generic DeepSeek V4 thinking wrapper from injecting unsupported `payload.thinking` on the Foundry-hosted OpenAI-compatible route. Without that, the real CLI proof for the DSML fix was blocked earlier by `400 Unrecognized request argument supplied: thinking` before tool execution could even be exercised.

## What changed
- recover complete DeepSeek DSML `tool_calls` text blocks into synthetic `toolCall` events in `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts`
- preserve visible text around recovered DSML markup
- emit every valid recovered `invoke` entry in a DSML block
- keep malformed or incomplete DSML non-executable
- skip the generic DeepSeek V4 thinking wrapper on the `microsoft-foundry` fallback path so the real Foundry route can be exercised without the earlier request-shaping failure
- add focused regression tests for both the DSML recovery behavior and the Foundry fallback-path guard

## Real runtime proof
### Environment
- machine: local macOS operator environment
- runtime path exercised: local CLI via source checkout at `/Users/manos/Code/openclaw`
- patched build tested: `OpenClaw 2026.5.24 (5f09fa9)`
- exact commit tested: `5f09fa9935943402805d39ea2d1e280b5a45249d`
- provider/model exercised: `microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro`

### Before
Installed CLI on this machine before using the patched build:
- `openclaw --version` -> `OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (a374c3a)`

Same live tool-use command on the installed binary failed on the affected Foundry route with:
- `400 Unrecognized request argument supplied: thinking`

That showed the route was still blocked before tool execution on the older local runtime.

### After
Patched build command:
```bash
node /Users/manos/Code/openclaw/openclaw.mjs agent --local --agent main --model microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro --thinking minimal --message 'Call the session_status tool exactly once with sessionKey current and then stop.' --json
```

Observed result on the patched build:
- request completed successfully on `microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro`
- `toolSummary.calls` = `1`
- `toolSummary.tools` = `["session_status"]`
- final assistant visible text: `Current model: \`microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro\`.`
- execution trace showed a successful single-attempt run on the requested provider/model with no fallback

This proves the real Foundry tool-use surface now gets past the earlier request-shaping failure and actually executes the requested tool on the patched build.

### Remaining proof gap
I did **not** capture the raw provider stream for the successful patched run, so I cannot honestly claim from this proof artifact alone whether Foundry emitted native `tool_calls` or DSML text that then flowed through the recovery path. What I can claim is narrower and true:
- this exact real CLI route previously failed to reach tool execution locally
- on the patched build it now successfully executes the requested tool and returns grounded output from that tool
- focused unit coverage still directly exercises the DSML-text recovery path itself, including multiple invokes and visible-text preservation

## Original live evidence for the DSML symptom
Installed runtime checked locally before the patched-build proof work:
- `openclaw --version` -> `OpenClaw 2026.5.20 (e510042)`

Tool-use repro against the real CLI/runtime:
```bash
openclaw agent --local --agent main --model microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro --thinking minimal --message 'Call the session_status tool exactly once with sessionKey current and then stop.' --json
```

Observed assistant payload returned as visible text instead of an executed tool call:
```xml
<｜DSML｜tool_calls>
<｜DSML｜invoke name="session_status">
<｜DSML｜parameter name="sessionKey" string="true">current</｜DSML｜parameter>
</｜DSML｜invoke>
</｜DSML｜tool_calls>
```

A second live variant was malformed/incomplete and omitted the parameter body entirely:
```xml
<｜DSML｜tool_calls>
<｜DSML｜invoke name="session_status">
</｜DSML｜invoke>
</｜DSML｜tool_calls>
```

Subagent/history verification also showed that the tool was not actually executed; the markup was emitted as assistant text.

## Source diagnosis
Relevant repo inspected locally at `/Users/manos/Code/openclaw`.

### What the current code did before this patch
`src/agents/deepseek-text-filter.ts` strips DSML wrapper text from visible output.

`src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` used that filter in `processOpenAICompletionsStream(...)`, but executable tool calls still came only from native `choiceDelta.tool_calls`.

So the prior behavior was:
- if DeepSeek/Foundry emitted native `tool_calls`, OpenClaw could handle them
- if DeepSeek/Foundry emitted DSML tool markup as plain text, OpenClaw filtered/streamed text but did not promote it to `toolCall`

### Why this is likely not just expected model behavior
DeepSeek's own tool-calls docs describe OpenAI-compatible native `message.tool_calls` as the intended path:
- https://api-docs.deepseek.com/guides/tool_calls

There is also upstream evidence that DeepSeek V4 Pro can intermittently emit tool calls as plain text with `tool_calls: null`:
- https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3/issues/1244

So the remaining problem looks like a combination of:
1. upstream/provider instability in DeepSeek tool emission
2. missing defensive recovery in OpenClaw for DSML/textual tool intent on the `openai-completions` stream path

## Validation
Targeted tests run locally:
```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts -t 'DeepSeek DSML|tool calls'
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts -t 'DeepSeek V4|MiMo V2.6'
git diff --check
pnpm build
```

Results:
- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`: 2 files passed, 18 tests passed, 374 skipped
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts`: 2 files passed, 6 tests passed, 250 skipped
- `git diff --check`: clean
- `pnpm build`: succeeded

## Labels / framing suggestion
- bug
- providers
- microsoft-foundry
- deepseek
- streaming
- tool-calls

target.title: fix(cli): preserve Discord voice outbound helper
target.body:
Summary
- Keep CLI lazy outbound dependency routing from treating Discord voice helper keys as normal channel adapters.
- Preserve explicitly supplied `discordVoice` / `sendDiscordVoice` helpers while leaving missing helpers unresolved so the Discord runtime can decide the voice path.
- Add focused regression coverage for the CLI deps proxy and outbound send mapping for #84952.

Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/deps.test.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 4 files, 109 tests passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/cli/deps.ts src/cli/deps.test.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.test.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/cli/deps.ts src/cli/deps.test.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.test.ts`
- `git diff --check refs/remotes/upstream-safe/main...HEAD`
- `git diff --check`

Signed head: `ea165135f487c71407f07008c6f6cbc2b30c8a24`.

Behavior addressed: Discord cron announces with `messages.tts.auto="always"` could fail before the Discord voice-message helper ran because the CLI outbound deps proxy manufactured a fake channel adapter for the `discordVoice` helper lookup.

Real environment tested: WSL2 Ubuntu source checkout on Node 22 with focused CLI, Discord outbound adapter, and cron double-announce regression tests. Crabbox/live Discord delivery was not rerun for this refresh.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/deps.test.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts --reporter=dot`

Evidence after fix: The CLI deps regression asserts `createDefaultDeps().discordVoice` and `createDefaultDeps().sendDiscordVoice` remain `undefined` instead of becoming unavailable channel adapters, and the outbound mapping regression asserts lazy channel factories are not called for those helper keys while explicit helpers are still preserved.

Observed result after fix: Focused regression suite passed: 4 files, 109 tests passed. Formatting, lint, diff checks, and the branch was rebased onto current `main` with an SSH-signed head commit.

What was not tested: A fresh live Discord cron announce with generated TTS audio was not run in this refresh; the existing live Discord proof remains in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85529#issuecomment-4529709599.

Fixes #84952

target.title: Add Eden AI provider plugin
target.body:
## Summary

Add Eden AI as a bundled provider plugin.

- Problem: OpenClaw users who want to route through Eden AI's meta-gateway (349 models, 21 underlying vendors) currently have to write a manual `models.providers.edenai` config and pick a base URL by hand. There is no bundled plugin, no `--auth-choice`, no docs page.
- Why it matters: Eden AI is positioned alongside OpenRouter and Vercel AI Gateway as one of the few OpenAI-compatible meta-gateways. Users already comfortable with `openclaw onboard --auth-choice openrouter-api-key` expect the same flow for Eden AI.
- What changed: a new bundled plugin at `extensions/edenai/`, mirroring `extensions/openrouter/` minus the speech / image / video / media capabilities. Text inference only. Includes onboarding, static eight-model catalog, live `/v3/models` discovery via the OpenAI-compatible transport, `EDENAI_API_KEY` auth, `--edenai-api-key` CLI flag, `edenai-api-key` wizard choice, aliases (`eden`, `eden-ai`, `eden_ai`), Mintlify provider page, `.env.example` row, and a labeler entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no edits to `src/**`, no changes to `CHANGELOG.md`, no other extension directory touched, no embeddings or image/audio/speech capabilities (those are follow-up PRs).

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: bundled Eden AI provider plugin so `openclaw onboard --auth-choice edenai-api-key` and `openclaw chat --provider edenai` work out of the box.
- Real environment tested: macOS / Linux dev shell, OpenClaw built from this branch (`pnpm build` clean).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  export EDENAI_API_KEY=...           # real key from app.edenai.run/admin/account/settings
  pnpm openclaw onboard --non-interactive \
      --auth-choice edenai-api-key \
      --edenai-api-key "$EDENAI_API_KEY"
  pnpm openclaw models list --provider edenai | head
  pnpm openclaw chat --provider edenai --model edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 -q "Reply with exactly OK."
  OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 EDENAI_API_KEY="$EDENAI_API_KEY" \
      pnpm test extensions/edenai/edenai.live.test.ts
  ```
- Evidence after fix: see the screenshot/terminal capture attached to the PR. Live `chat/completions` returns `OK`, the live `/v3/models` catalog (500+ rows) is warmed by `prepareDynamicModel` and feeds dynamic resolution with real per-model metadata, and the gated live vitest passes.
- Observed result after fix: provider is selectable from the wizard; `openclaw chat` returns a real assistant reply from multiple underlying vendors via Eden AI; `openclaw models list --provider edenai` shows the curated offline catalog while live discovery powers the runtime model resolution path.
- What was not tested: image generation, audio transcription, TTS, and embeddings (intentionally out of scope for this PR; see Out of scope below). Codex review was not run locally because the contributor does not have Codex access.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A (feature add, not a bug fix).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/edenai/onboard.test.ts`, `extensions/edenai/provider-catalog.test.ts`, `extensions/edenai/edenai.live.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: provider registers under id `edenai` with `EDENAI_API_KEY` auth, the static catalog ships exactly eight models with Eden AI's hyphen Anthropic id form, `normalizeEdenaiBaseUrl` canonicalises `/v3` and legacy `/v2` paths, onboard preserves the alias and primary model, and dynamic model resolution hits Eden AI's live `/v3/chat/completions` end to end (gated).
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: catalog drift between Eden AI's published model ids and the static fallback is the most likely silent failure; the eight-entry test asserts the hyphen ids verbatim. Base-URL normalisation is the next-most-likely regression as Eden AI's API moves from `/v2` to `/v3`.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New auth choice in the onboarding wizard: "Eden AI API key" (group "Eden AI").
- New CLI flag `--edenai-api-key <key>` and direct env var `EDENAI_API_KEY`.
- New model-ref shape `edenai/<vendor>/<model>` (for example `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`).
- Anthropic models on Eden AI use the hyphen id form (`claude-opus-4-6`), not the dot form (`claude-opus-4.6`). The bundled catalog uses the hyphen form Eden AI returns; Eden AI rejects the dot form with HTTP 400. Manual `models.providers.edenai.models[].id` entries should follow Eden AI's form, not the upstream vendor's.
- Eden AI wraps `response_format: {"type": "json_object"}` output in a ` ```json ... ``` ` code fence. OpenClaw's parser does not require strict `JSON.parse` on assistant text, so this is cosmetic; consumers that need raw JSON should strip the fence.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user]
   |
   v
manual models.providers.edenai = { baseUrl, apiKey, api: "openai-completions", models: [...] }

After:
[user] -> openclaw onboard --auth-choice edenai-api-key
              |
              v
        bundled plugin (extensions/edenai)
              |
              v
        OpenAI-compatible /v3/chat/completions -> 21 underlying vendors
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - reuses the existing `EDENAI_API_KEY` env var convention via the shared `createProviderApiKeyAuthMethod` helper.
- New/changed network calls? Yes - outbound `GET https://api.edenai.run/v3/models` (during live catalog discovery) and the standard `POST /v3/chat/completions` for inference. Both go to a single host suffix (`api.edenai.run`).
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No - users explicitly opt in by setting their own key.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the network calls share the same SSRF guard, timeout, and error handling that every other OpenAI-compatible provider plugin uses (via `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`). Eden AI's endpoint is publicly documented and the only outbound host is `api.edenai.run`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux x86_64 (also exercised on macOS arm64).
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0.
- Model/provider: `edenai` (default model `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`).
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a (provider plugin only).
- Relevant config (redacted): `EDENAI_API_KEY=ek_...` in `~/.openclaw/.env`.

### Steps

1. `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test extensions/edenai/`
2. `pnpm openclaw onboard --non-interactive --auth-choice edenai-api-key --edenai-api-key "$EDENAI_API_KEY"`
3. `pnpm openclaw models list --provider edenai`
4. `pnpm openclaw chat --provider edenai --model edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 -q "Reply with exactly OK."`

### Expected

- `pnpm check` clean (typecheck, oxlint, contract checks).
- `pnpm test extensions/edenai` shows 6 unit tests passing (live test skipped when `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST` is unset).
- Step 2 prints the configured provider and selected default model.
- Step 3 prints the eight bundled static entries plus the full live catalog when the key resolves.
- Step 4 returns `OK` (or `OK.`/`OK!`).

### Actual

- `pnpm check` clean (lint, typecheck, runtime sidecar guard, dependency pin guard, runtime import cycles - all green).
- `pnpm test:extension edenai` -> 2 test files, 6 tests passing in \~4 s; live test correctly skipped.
- Live chat with `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` returns `OK.` - see attached terminal capture.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new vitest files; no prior coverage to compare against).
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording (terminal capture of `openclaw chat --provider edenai`, attached as a PR comment because GitHub does not let contributors inline-attach artifacts in the description).
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI):

- Verified scenarios: `openclaw onboard --auth-choice edenai-api-key` flow with a real key; `models list --provider edenai` against the live catalog; `chat` with `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` reaching Eden AI and getting an assistant reply.
- Edge cases checked: missing key falls back to the static eight-model catalog (no network); `EDENAI_API_KEY=invalid` surfaces the upstream HTTP 401 cleanly through OpenClaw's standard provider error path.
- What you did **not** verify: image-generation, audio, TTS, and embeddings capabilities (deliberately out of scope; see "Out of scope" in the diagram block).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes - additive only.
- Config/env changes? Yes - new optional env var `EDENAI_API_KEY` and CLI flag `--edenai-api-key`; both unset by default and have no effect on existing setups.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: n/a.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Eden AI's live `/v3/models` catalog includes ids that are not yet in the static fallback list. The static list (eight curated entries) only fires when `EDENAI_API_KEY` is unset.
  - Mitigation: `resolveDynamicModel` accepts any `edenai/<vendor>/<model>` id and synthesises a `ProviderRuntimeModel` with sensible defaults if the live catalog hasn't been fetched yet. Behavior matches OpenRouter's `resolveDynamicModel` path.
- Risk: future Eden AI gateway updates could move endpoints again (the plugin already handles the `/v2` -> `/v3` rename via `normalizeEdenaiBaseUrl`).
  - Mitigation: tests assert the canonical and known-legacy base URLs both normalise to `/v3`. Adding a new legacy variant is a single-line change.

## Out of scope (follow-ups)

- Embeddings via `registerEmbeddingProvider` (Eden AI exposes `/v3/embeddings`).
- Image generation via `registerImageGenerationProvider` (`/v3/image/generations`).
- Audio transcription via `registerMediaUnderstandingProvider({ capabilities: ["audio"] })` (`/v3/audio/transcriptions`).
- Text-to-speech via `registerSpeechProvider` (`/v3/audio/speech`).
- Reasoning policy (`resolveThinkingProfile`): Eden AI does not currently forward Anthropic's `thinking` budget or OpenAI's reasoning-effort knob in a uniform way. Defer until Eden AI exposes the parameter.

## Summary

Skips wildcard default model allowlist entries while building concrete model catalog metadata and configured `models list` rows.

Entries such as `openai-codex/*` are valid allowlist selectors, but they are not concrete model ids and do not have per-model alias metadata to resolve or display as configured rows. The catalog metadata builder and configured list resolver now ignore those wildcard keys instead of sending them through concrete model normalization.

Split out from #85817 so the policy-scoping PR remains policy-only.

target.title: fix: preserve subagent delivery after lock stalls
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserves suspended subagent final-delivery text into durable completion fallback state before expiring the outbox payload, so expiry is not a hard loss of the child result.
- Distinguishes expiry after durable fallback preservation from true payload discard.
- Adds session write-lock timeout owner diagnostics including liveness, starttime, current starttime, age, and stale reasons.
- Out of scope: redesigning subagent scheduling or session JSONL lock ownership.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #86537
Closes #86538

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #86537
Related #86538

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Local bug evidence under `/mnt/c/OpenClaw/bugs/BUG-035-subagent-terminal-reconciliation-times-out-then-discards-delivery` and `/mnt/c/OpenClaw/bugs/BUG-036-session-write-lock-timeouts-block-main-and-subagent-lanes`.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Expired suspended subagent final delivery now keeps final text durably instead of dropping the only payload, and write-lock timeouts expose actionable owner diagnostics.
- Real environment tested: Linux WSL2 dev checkout plus Azure Crabbox remote validation. Before proof used Azure Crabbox `silver-prawn` / `cbx_e06ef86ae274`; after proof used Azure Crabbox `violet-lobster` / `cbx_3eda7a42d715`.

target.title: refactor: use Rastermill for image processing
target.body:
## Summary

- Move OpenClaw image processing to the published npm package `rastermill@0.1.0`.
- Delete the in-repo image processor implementation from `src/media/image-ops.ts`; OpenClaw now uses a small Rastermill adapter while keeping trusted native command resolution.
- Preserve input/output pixel limits, `OPENCLAW_IMAGE_BACKEND`, Windows/native backend aliases, best-effort EXIF normalization, and central availability-error classification.

## Verification

- Rastermill npm: `npm view rastermill@0.1.0 name version dist.tarball dist.integrity dist.unpackedSize dependencies --json`
- Rastermill package before publish: `pnpm check && pnpm test && pnpm build && npm pack --dry-run`
- Rastermill publish: `npm publish --access public` published `rastermill@0.1.0`
- Rastermill contract follow-up: `pnpm test`; `pnpm check && pnpm build` after adding the backend fallback-stop test on `openclaw/rastermill@4045362`
- OpenClaw focused tests: `pnpm test src/media/image-ops.security.test.ts src/media/image-ops.rastermill-adapter.test.ts src/media/image-ops.input-guard.test.ts src/media/image-ops.helpers.test.ts src/media/web-media.test.ts src/agents/tool-images.test.ts src/gateway/managed-image-attachments.test.ts test/package-manager-config.test.ts test/scripts/check-dependency-pins.test.ts test/scripts/generate-npm-shrinkwrap.test.ts`
- OpenClaw TUI PTY: `timeout 120s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts`
- OpenClaw build: `env -u OPENCLAW_TESTBOX pnpm build`
- Auto review: `autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: OpenClaw no longer owns its image-processing Photon implementation directly; core media image operations delegate to the public npm package `rastermill@0.1.0` while preserving safety and backend behavior.
Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, Node/pnpm repo defaults, real `rastermill@0.1.0` dependency fetched from npm.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm install --lockfile-only`; `node scripts/generate-npm-shrinkwrap.mjs`; focused `pnpm test ...`; `timeout 120s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts`; `env -u OPENCLAW_TESTBOX pnpm build`; `autoreview --mode local`; npm registry verification for `rastermill@0.1.0`; Rastermill package `pnpm test`, `pnpm check`, and `pnpm build`.
Evidence after fix: npm reports `rastermill@0.1.0` at `https://registry.npmjs.org/rastermill/-/rastermill-0.1.0.tgz` with integrity `sha512-ovbl5jVRMc0LnzktQMU13Nz5c9iPbHi9PoT4hG8qiyOGVM6a20I6N2TtaFJv40B38LLF29luN/E8NmNIXk7lCg==`; focused OpenClaw tests passed 5 Vitest shards; TUI PTY passed 11 tests; build completed; Rastermill tests/check/build passed; autoreview was clean.
Observed result after fix: image metadata/resizing/PNG optimization/agent attachment paths remain green, native fallback commands still route through OpenClaw trusted resolution, shrinkwraps resolve the public npm package, and the TUI fixture can resolve Rastermill through tsx/Node loader probing. Rastermill now has a package-level contract test proving real Photon processing errors do not fall through to native fallback tools.
What was not tested: Windows `windows-native` PowerShell execution was preserved in Rastermill and typechecked, but not live-run on a Windows host in this local pass. `pnpm check:changed` currently reaches unrelated latest-main type errors in `src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts` and `src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts`; those files are not changed by this PR.

## Summary

- Avoid the voice-call plugin's duplicate OpenAI realtime greeting by making proactive/manual OpenAI speech an instructed `response.create` instead of injecting a synthetic user message.

target.title: Fix plugin runtime module resolution diagnostics
target.body:
## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed:
Packaged plugin runtime resolution no longer fails silently when the package-root marker path is unavailable, and any remaining failure includes loader, package-root, candidate-path, plugin id, runtime property, and plugin source context.

Real environment tested:
Local macOS shell, Node v24.15.0, built and packed OpenClaw from this branch, then installed published `openclaw@2026.5.20` into a fresh temp npm prefix and upgraded that same prefix to the local packed `openclaw@2026.5.25` build.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `npm install --prefix <temp>/prefix openclaw@2026.5.20`
2. `npm install --prefix <temp>/prefix <repo>/openclaw-2026.5.25.tgz`
3. Loaded a no-secret `proof-channel` plugin from the upgraded package with `loadOpenClawPlugins`; the plugin reads `api.runtime.version` during register and registers a configured channel account.
4. Imported the packed package's generated `server-channels-*` module, created a channel manager, and ran `manager.startChannels()` with startup tracing enabled.

Evidence after fix:
The package-level proof printed:
```json
{
  "baselineVersion": "2026.5.20",
```

## Summary

- Add structured diagnostics for `resolvePluginRuntimeModulePath`, including loader path, package root, candidates, and resolver errors.
- Add an ancestor candidate fallback so packaged/runtime-cache contexts can still find `dist/plugins/runtime/index.js` even if package-root marker resolution misses.
- Preserve plugin id/property/source context when a plugin triggers lazy runtime resolution and the host runtime module still cannot be found.

Closes #86117

target.title: feat(zalouser): add groupLink action to fetch a group's invite link
target.body:
## What
Adds a `groupLink` action to the zalouser tool that returns a group's shareable invite link. The tool can already `groups` (list) and `link` (send a URL as a message), but there was no way to read a group's invite link.

## Changes
- `zca-client.ts`: add `getGroupLinkDetail(groupId)` to the `API` interface.
- `zalo-js.ts`: add `getZaloGroupInviteLink(profile, groupId)` wrapping it via `withZaloApi`, returning `{ link?, expiresAt?, enabled }`.
- `tool.ts`: add the `groupLink` action (resolves the group thread, returns the link detail).
- `tool.test.ts`: success + disabled-link + missing-group-id cases.

## Why getGroupLinkDetail (not enableGroupLink)
`enableGroupLink` toggles the invite-link feature on, which is admin-only — a regular member account gets a permission error. `getGroupLinkDetail` reads the existing link, so it works for any member once a group admin has enabled the link. When the link is not enabled the action returns `{ enabled: false }` and the caller degrades cleanly.

## Use case
Lets an agent (e.g. a customer-service hand-off flow) surface a link so a human can join the relevant group.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: the groupLink action originally called the admin-only enableGroupLink, so a regular (non-admin) group member hit a Zalo permission error ("Không đủ quyền") and never got a link. After switching to the read-only getGroupLinkDetail, a non-admin member reads an already-enabled invite link.

Real environment tested: a real Zalo personal account signed in as a regular member (not an admin) of the target groups, running inside a live OpenClaw agent container. The bundled @openclaw/zalouser plugin was hot-patched on disk to mirror this branch and the gateway was restarted so the patched module loaded.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: docker exec <agent> openclaw agent --message "Call the zalouser tool with action=groupLink, threadId=<group id> and return the raw JSON result" --json — run against three real groups (two with the invite link enabled, one with it disabled), driving the patched groupLink action end-to-end through tool.ts to zalo-js to zca-js getGroupLinkDetail.

Evidence after fix: live output copied from the running container for an enabled group (invite-link token redacted; it is a real join link to a private group):
```json
{ "link": "https://zalo.me/g/<redacted>", "expiresAt": 1937494664737, "enabled": true }
```
the same openclaw groupLink call against a group whose invite link was disabled returned, with no error:
```json
{ "enabled": false }
```

Observed result after fix: across three real groups the non-admin member account read invite links via getGroupLinkDetail — two enabled groups returned valid https://zalo.me/g/<token> links with an expiry, and the disabled group returned { enabled: false }. With the previous enableGroupLink call every one of those groups returned "Không đủ quyền". The docker exec openclaw run printed the JSON shown above verbatim.

What was not tested: the admin-only enableGroupLink toggle path (out of scope — this PR is read-only); the downstream hand-off message wiring that consumes the link lives in a separate consumer, not this PR.

- add a Windows-only respawn path that launches the CLI with `--stack-size=8192`

## Validation
- WSL/Linux: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/entry.respawn.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 16 tests passed

## Windows packaged runtime proof

Head tested: `b139bf5db3f900ff694ac3e376855c84185091ed`.

Environment: Windows 11, Node `v22.22.3`, npm `10.9.8`, package tarball built from this head with `npm pack` after `node scripts/build-all.mjs`.

Local machine caveat: this Windows environment blocks Node from executing script filenames containing `openclaw` with `Access is denied`, even for a one-line test file. To avoid that local endpoint-policy block while still testing the packed runtime, I extracted the tarball under `C:\src\claws\ocpkg-86307\pkg`, installed production dependencies in that extracted package with `npm install --ignore-scripts --omit=dev --no-bin-links`, and invoked the packed `dist\entry.js` directly. This exercises the PR's Windows `buildCliRespawnPlan()` / `runCliRespawnPlan()` path without manually setting `NODE_OPTIONS`.

Exact proof commands/results:

- `NODE_DEBUG=child_process node C:\src\claws\ocpkg-86307\pkg\dist\entry.js plugins list --json` -> exit `0`; debug output showed the Windows respawn args included `C:\Program Files\nodejs\node.exe`, `--stack-size=8192`, the packed `dist\entry.js`, and `plugins list --json`.

commands.author: ragesaq

target.title: AI-assisted: fix(chat): bound chat history display payloads
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes `chat.history` display payload growth by adding bounded display projections before history responses are returned to callers.

It covers text, tool payloads, image/media blocks, canvas media references, Swift protocol surfaces, UI/controller handling, gateway tests, public protocol docs, and a beta2 fix report.

AI-assisted change: yes. Forge/Codex produced the implementation and PR publication using OpenAI Codex `gpt-5.5` in the OpenClaw workspace. Operator reviewed the findings, evidence packet, and remediation plan before this update.

## Problem

`chat.history` could return oversized visible history payloads when transcripts contained large tool results, media blocks, or long message content. That created an availability risk for WebChat and gateway callers because history reads could become large enough to stall UI/session flows.

A late review pass also found that normal `messages` mode was capped to 64 KiB after safe tool redaction, which could unnecessarily drop safe text even when the caller configured a larger history budget.

## Root Cause

History projection and redaction were not consistently separated from raw transcript preservation. The display path needed bounded, caller-safe projections while keeping raw transcript data out of normal UI responses.

The final `messages` mode budget also applied a hard 64 KiB cap after redaction, rather than honoring the configured history budget for safe text.

## Solution

- Adds bounded chat display projection helpers for text, tool payloads, media, and canvas blocks.
- Routes `chat.history` display responses through bounded projections instead of exposing unbounded raw payload shape.
- Adds `mode: "messages" | "turns" | "raw-messages"` and gates raw payload inspection behind `operator.admin`.
- Preserves safe `messages` mode text up to the configured `maxHistoryBytes` budget after tool payload omission.
- Regenerates Swift protocol models from the canonical schema output.
- Documents the new history modes and `unsafeRawToolPayloads` admin guard in public WebChat/Gateway protocol docs.
- Adds gateway regression tests for bounded tool payloads, visible media shaping, recent visible turn preservation, placeholder semantics, and large safe text preservation.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `chat.history` was returning oversized display payloads (production logs showed >=88 KB responses and `readMs=1715-1893` even for tiny sessions, plus an 11050 ms WebSocket response), and could leak raw tool-call arguments / tool-result payloads into display history. This PR bounds display payloads, collapses tool turns by default, gates raw payloads behind `operator.admin`, and adds `mode=messages|turns|raw-messages` with omitted-mode defaulting to `turns` for display clients.

**Real environment tested:** Isolated child Gateway booted from the PR source tree at `f626813d8a8b5f3faa38a0674099906cef8ca454` on `forge/chat-history-display-bounds-v2`. Host `openclaw-prod` local shell, Node `v22.22.2`, ephemeral test token. Production Gateway not touched. Channels, providers, cron, and bundled plugins disabled in the child process. Transcript fixture is synthetic and redacted: 108 lines, 1,084,592 bytes, with embedded large tool results, assistant tool-call arguments, a canvas reference, and a media-path reference; marker strings used to detect raw-payload leakage. The child gateway ran with `gateway.controlUi.dangerouslyAllowHostHeaderOriginFallback=true` and `gateway.controlUi.allowInsecureAuth=true`; those are test-harness break-glass flags only for 127.0.0.1 loopback, not changed runtime defaults under exercise.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```bash
PATH=/tmp/openclaw-proof-bin:$PATH \
  ./node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/openclaw-chat-history-proof-86458.mts \
  > /tmp/openclaw-chat-history-proof-86458-after-f626813d.json
```
The harness boots an isolated Gateway, opens real WebSocket RPC clients with distinct client IDs, and calls `chat.history` against the oversized fixture for each surface: WebChat, TUI, macOS, iOS, Android, generic UI, CLI, plus explicit `mode=messages`, non-admin `raw-messages` (must reject), and admin `raw-messages` (must succeed).

**Evidence after fix:** Raw JSON `/tmp/openclaw-chat-history-proof-86458-after-f626813d.json` (190 lines, includes per-client results and Gateway stderr/stdout tail with the `chat.history metrics` lines from the actual run). Representative metric line copied from the isolated PR Gateway log:
```text
chat.history metrics mode=turns responseMode=turns rawToolPayloads=false sessionHash=0d6e4079e367 requestedLimit=50 max=50 scanMax=1000 maxChars=4000 maxHistoryBytes=6291456 finalHistoryBudgetBytes=6291456 localMessages=108 rawMessages=108 projectedMessages=108 normalizedMessages=27 returnedMessages=27 returnedItems=9 rawMessagesMatched=108 toolRecordsCollapsed=45 hasMoreBefore=false placeholders=0 safeToolPlaceholders=0 safeToolOmittedBytes=0 messagesBytesReturned=6438 responseBytesReturned=13092 sessionKnown=true transcriptRead=true readMs=12 readFileBytes=1084592 readBytes=1084592 readLines=108 readMode=full-tail totalMs=25
```
Production before-evidence from the same affected surface, redacted to session hashes, from `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-05-24.log`:
```text
2026-05-24T23:13:35.952Z chat.history metrics mode=messages sessionHash=ac38af406fed ... messagesBytesReturned=87915 responseBytesReturned=88104 readMs=1838 totalMs=1840
2026-05-24T23:13:35.947Z chat.history metrics mode=messages sessionHash=75794a8e1d45 ... messagesBytesReturned=57617 responseBytesReturned=57810 readMs=1890 totalMs=1893
2026-05-24T23:01:46.498Z chat.history metrics mode=messages sessionHash=a6923f4da653 ... localMessages=0 messagesBytesReturned=2 responseBytesReturned=128 readMs=1715 totalMs=1716
2026-05-24T06:19:28.690Z WebSocket chat.history response took 11050ms
```

**Observed result after fix:** On the PR build, omitted-mode display clients (WebChat, TUI, macOS, iOS, Android, generic UI) returned `mode=turns`, 27 normalized messages collapsed to 9 display items, 13,092 response bytes, no raw tool markers, no raw assistant tool-argument markers, safe visible text and canvas reference preserved, 45 tool records collapsed, `totalMs` 10-25 ms across repeated calls on the 1,084,592-byte transcript. Explicit `mode=messages` returned 50 messages, 12,118 bytes, no raw markers, `[chat.history tool payload omitted]` placeholders present. Non-admin `mode=raw-messages` rejected with Gateway code `INVALID_REQUEST` and message `chat.history raw-messages requires operator.admin scope`. Admin `mode=raw-messages` returned 50 messages and 494,324 bytes with raw markers present. CLI omitted-mode intentionally still defaults to `mode=messages` to preserve gateway-callable legacy semantics.

**Not tested:**
- Control UI / WebChat browser surface was not driven through a browser or screen-recording session in this proof. Compatibility was exercised at the Gateway WebSocket protocol layer with the WebChat client ID, not via DOM render.
- Same-fixture live run against the merge-base build was attempted, but the base worktree's `pnpm install` aborted non-interactively against a symlinked `node_modules`. Before evidence is therefore real production log lines from the affected surface rather than a same-fixture old-build replay.

## Validation

| Command / Gate | Result | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| Focused chat history regression suite | PASS | Earlier closeout run: 4 files, 142 tests |
| `corepack pnpm vitest run src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts` | PASS | Fresh post-review run: 2 files, 132 tests |
| `CI=true corepack pnpm check:changed` | PASS | Fresh post-review run; includes local deadcode/dependency gate and notes SwiftLint is macOS-CI owned |
| Swift protocol generation | PASS | `GatewayModels.swift` regenerated to canonical output |
| `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts -t "chat.history messages mode omits raw tool payloads\|chat.history messages mode ignores unsafeRawToolPayloads\|chat.history raw-messages requires an admin or debug caller\|chat.history raw-messages preserves raw tool payloads"` | PASS | 4 tests pass at `bc42a3f2d0`; covers the new `mode: "messages"` + `unsafeRawToolPayloads: true` regression |
| Real behavior proof parser shape | PASS EXPECTED | This PR body now uses the required `## Real behavior proof` field labels and embeds non-mock command/log evidence |

## Review Findings Addressed

- ClawSweeper real-behavior-proof block: replaced prose proof with parser-shaped live evidence from an isolated Gateway run.
- ClawSweeper P2 docs finding: documented `chat.history` modes and `unsafeRawToolPayloads` guard in `docs/web/webchat.md` and `docs/gateway/protocol.md`.
- Bundled Swift protocol check: regenerated `GatewayModels.swift` instead of hand-editing optional defaults.
- Agent-chat tests: updated the gateway-callable helper expectations so placeholder redaction does not break semantic assertions.
- Dependency/deadcode check: fresh `check:changed` passes locally; the prior `ui/src/ui/control-ui-chunking.ts` orphan failure appears stale relative to this head.
- Copilot hot-path nits: avoided a second `augmentChatHistoryWithCanvasBlocks()` pass for non-raw history responses and cached `extractToolCallIds()` in turn aggregation.
- Saturated scan-window `meta.hasMoreBefore` handling: fixed in `19b41bf4a2` with a 1001-turn regression test verifying earlier history is reported when the scan window saturates.
- ClawSweeper P2 `unsafeRawToolPayloads` contract: raw-payload branch now requires `mode === "raw-messages"` (`bc42a3f2d0`). Admin callers using `mode: "messages"` with the flag get the safe display projection. Added Gateway regression test `chat.history messages mode ignores unsafeRawToolPayloads for admin callers`.
- ClawSweeper P2 unused-binding follow-up: removed the unused `unsafeRawToolPayloads` destructure in `7e2acc2b83`. The params schema still accepts the field for backwards compatibility and the `mode === "raw-messages"` guard is unchanged.
- CI i18n drift: synced `ui/src/i18n/.i18n/raw-copy-baseline.json` to main's updated compaction-divider description in `65e4117bd2` (after merging main into the branch). This unblocks `build-artifacts` -> `Check Control UI i18n`.
- ClawSweeper P2 `unsafeRawToolPayloads` contract finding (`src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts:2177`): fixed in `bc42a3f2d0`. The raw-payload branch now requires `mode === "raw-messages"`, so admin callers that send `mode: "messages", unsafeRawToolPayloads: true` are routed through the safe display projection and the flag is silently ignored for `messages` and `turns`. The runtime now matches the documented contract in `docs/web/webchat.md` and `docs/gateway/protocol.md`. Added Gateway regression test `chat.history messages mode ignores unsafeRawToolPayloads for admin callers` covering the previously ambiguous path.

## Remaining Follow-Up

- The real proof did not include a browser screenshot or recording. WebSocket-level client compatibility is covered here; UI visual proof can be added if maintainers want browser evidence.

## Risk Before Merge

- Compatibility: `chat.history` response shape is intentionally bounded for display modes. Raw/debug access remains behind the explicit admin raw path.
- Message delivery / UI availability: this reduces large-history pressure, but maintainers should still watch WebChat history latency on beta2.
- Session-state: history display output changes should be checked against existing UI expectations and Swift clients.

## Changelog

No `CHANGELOG.md` entry was added. OpenClaw contributor guidance leaves changelog handling to maintainers or landing automation.
